surface.name: React Chat connected client with tutorial credentials
surface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: React Chat connected client with tutorial credentials. The current React Chat tutorial provides generated credentials and an explicit earliest connection checkpoint before channel and message UI work. It is a complete low-dependency candidate, not a platform-wide default over Stream's other products or SDKs. Prior surface before policy commit: Stream product and SDK documentation selector.

alternatives_considered.name: Chat Messaging
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It has UI-component, client, server, REST, tutorial, sample-app, and agent-skill routes across many platforms, but is not selected over Stream's other products.

alternatives_considered.name: Video & Audio
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It offers seven client SDKs, Platform API, Vision Agents, example apps, several use cases, and prebuilt versus custom React paths without being the platform default.

alternatives_considered.name: Activity Feeds
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It offers client and server SDKs in both v3 and v2 and explicitly separates client-side from server-side responsibilities.

alternatives_considered.name: AI Moderation
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires an app and policy before a check, offers seven server SDKs, and is a separate outcome from chat, video, or feeds.

alternatives_considered.name: Vision Agents
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a separate Python and Gemini quickstart that needs both Stream and Google credentials and ends with a browser voice-agent interaction.

entry_point.developer_intent: Reach Stream's first useful documented developer success after selecting Stream as the platform.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Stream.
The developer has not selected Chat, Video & Audio, Activity Feeds, AI Moderation, or Vision Agents.
The developer has not selected a client platform, server language, UI kit, REST route, sample app, or agent-assisted route.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: The page displays Chat with client is ready! and the Network panel shows the secure WebSocket connection.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The developer first chooses a Stream product and implementation route, then completes that route's distinct observable success.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: Signals differ by route: a secure chat WebSocket and rendered ready state, a joined call with rendered participants, a created feed plus added Hello Stream Feeds activity, a keep/flag/remove moderation result, or a voice agent that greets the developer in the browser.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The page displays Chat with client is ready! and the Network panel shows the secure WebSocket connection.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: A product and route decision changes prerequisites, credentials, installation, code, external services, hardware permissions, and verification. Selecting any route as canonical would require human workshop judgment.

prerequisites.requirement: A Stream account and app are required for own-application routes. A single account provides access to Chat, Feeds, Video, Moderation, and Vision Agents; signup accepts email and password or Google or GitHub. Stream's React Chat tutorial can instead provide generated tutorial credentials.

Credential shape is route-specific: Chat uses API key plus user token, Video uses API key plus token or token provider, Feeds uses API key plus user token, Moderation uses app credentials, and Vision Agents uses Stream key and secret plus a Google AI Studio key.

Software depends on the selected SDK. Representative routes require a React toolchain and npm or yarn, a supported Feeds SDK, a server SDK for moderation, or uv with Python 3.10 through 3.13 for Vision Agents.

Video, Audio, and Vision Agent routes can require camera, microphone, browser permissions, and a user interaction before media playback; chat, feeds, and text moderation do not share this prerequisite.

Free entitlements are product-specific and must not be merged: Chat Build is free with no card and publishes 1,000 MAU plus 100 concurrent connections; every Video account receives $100 per month in free Audio/Video API usage with no card; Moderation provides $100 in free monthly credits for messages, images, and video with no card.

primary_path.action: Open Stream's developer documentation and inspect the product-level starting choices.
details: The common navigation lists Chat Messaging, Video & Audio, Activity Feeds, AI Moderation, and Vision Agents.
Chat, Video, and Feeds pages each advertise free coding without a credit card.
primary_path.success_signal: The developer can see that Stream does not expose a single product-neutral quickstart.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: No execution wait occurs at documentation arrival.

primary_path.action: Choose Chat Messaging, Video & Audio, Activity Feeds, AI Moderation, or Vision Agents, then choose that product's implementation route.
details: Chat offers React, iOS/Swift, Android, React Native, Flutter, Unreal, Unity, Angular, and JavaScript client or UI routes, plus NodeJS, Python, Ruby, PHP, Java, .NET, Go, and REST backend routes.
Video offers React, iOS/Swift, Android, React Native, Flutter, JavaScript, Unity, Platform API, and Vision Agents, with use-case choices including calls, audio rooms, livestreaming, voice, and AI.
Feeds v3 offers JavaScript, iOS, Android, React, React Native, Flutter clients and Node, Go, .NET, PHP, Java, Python servers, while v2 remains another documented route.
Moderation offers Node, Python, Go, Java, PHP, Ruby, and .NET server examples; Vision Agents uses its Python quickstart.
primary_path.input: Product outcome, target platform, language, UI-control preference, and authentication model
primary_path.output: A human-selected product and first-success route
primary_path.success_signal: One documented branch is selected for end-to-end execution.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The docs do not establish a universal default, so a canonical journey cannot continue without a human choice.

primary_path.action: For an own-application route, create or sign in to the common Stream account and choose Dashboard or Stream CLI onboarding.
details: Email and password, Google, and GitHub are documented account methods.
The signup flow asks for the main product to personalize onboarding, but the account can use multiple Stream products.
The React Chat tutorial's generated-credential route can bypass this own-account step.
primary_path.input: Account identity, selected main product, and Dashboard-versus-CLI preference
primary_path.output: Authenticated Stream account or documented generated-credential bypass
primary_path.success_signal: The Dashboard is open or getstream init is ready to authenticate.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The current official article does not enumerate email-verification or anti-abuse screens.

primary_path.action: Create, clone, pick, or temporarily provision the Stream application through the selected Dashboard or CLI path.
details: Dashboard creation asks for an application name and a server region for Chat and Feeds; the region is locked after creation.
Choose development or production mode. Stream recommends separate development and hardened production apps, and app settings can switch between those modes later.
An existing application can be cloned instead of creating a new one.

getstream init authenticates in a browser, selects an organization and existing or newly created app, and writes local credentials. getstream login --guest can create a temporary guest account backed by a throwaway app.
primary_path.input: Application name, immutable server-region choice where applicable, development or production mode, and optional source app to clone
primary_path.output: Stream application with API key and secret
primary_path.success_signal: The application is created or selected and its credentials are available to the Dashboard or local project.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A wrong server region cannot be changed after the Chat or Feeds app is created; production mode restricts destructive Dashboard actions.

primary_path.action: Open the new application's overview and verify its common setup output before product-specific code begins.
details: Dashboard creation routes to the app overview screen.
For Chat, the overview exposes API key and secret plus usage and configuration areas.
The CLI path writes .stream/creds.yaml and adds it to .gitignore automatically.
primary_path.success_signal: The overview is visible or local credential file exists, and the selected product quickstart can use the app.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Missing application credentials blocks own-app product routes.

primary_path.action: Confirm a React 19-compatible local toolchain and npm are available for the versions tested by the tutorial.
details: The tutorial states it was tested with React ^19 and Stream Chat SDK ^14; a configured CodeSandbox is an alternative.
primary_path.success_signal: The Vite React TypeScript scaffold can run locally.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Use the linked sandbox or correct the local Node, npm, or React environment before continuing.

primary_path.action: Run npm create vite chat-example -- --template react-ts, then cd chat-example.
primary_path.success_signal: The shell is inside the generated chat-example project.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Package, network, template, or filesystem errors must be corrected and the scaffold rerun.

primary_path.output: Installed Stream Chat client and React component packages
primary_path.success_signal: Both packages are installed in chat-example.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: npm, network, lockfile, or permissions errors must be corrected before imports resolve.

primary_path.action: Use the tutorial-generated apiKey, userId, userToken, and optional userName values shown with the code sample.
details: The API key identifies the generated Stream application; userId and userToken authorize the chat user.
Using an own Stream app and JWT generator is a separate branch.
primary_path.output: Temporary Chat application and user credentials
primary_path.success_signal: All required placeholder values can be replaced in App.tsx.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Expired, missing, copied incorrectly, or mismatched generated credentials prevent the client connection; refresh the tutorial values or use an own app and valid JWT.

primary_path.action: Replace src/App.tsx with the tutorial's initial Client Setup code and substitute the generated credentials.
details: The code imports Chat and useCreateChatClient, calls the hook once with apiKey, tokenOrProvider, and userData, renders Setting up client & connection... until ready, then renders Chat with the text Chat with client is ready!.
The tutorial warns that the client must establish a connection before it is passed to Chat.
primary_path.output: React app that creates and renders a connected Stream Chat client
primary_path.success_signal: App.tsx has no remaining credential placeholders and matches the initial setup.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: TypeScript, imports, placeholder, or credential errors must be corrected before the app can connect.

primary_path.output: Vite development server and Stream connection attempt
primary_path.success_signal: Vite prints a local site URL and the application loads without a terminal build error.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Build, dependency, port, or runtime errors must be corrected and the server restarted.

primary_path.action: Open the Vite site and verify both the ready text and a secure WebSocket connection to Stream in browser developer tools.
details: Stop at this first connected-client checkpoint; channels, message UI, styling, attachments, and channel lists are later tutorial sections.
primary_path.success_signal: The page displays Chat with client is ready! and the Network panel shows the secure WebSocket connection.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the page remains on Setting up client & connection... or no secure WebSocket appears, inspect browser errors, network access, package versions, and all generated credential values, then retry.

branches.condition: Chat Messaging with the current React tutorial is selected.
branches.path: Choose npm or yarn; scaffold the Vite React TypeScript chat-example project; install stream-chat and stream-chat-react; use the tutorial-generated apiKey, userId, userToken, and optional userName or supply an own Stream app and generated JWT; replace src/App.tsx with the useCreateChatClient setup; pass the connected client to Chat; run npm run dev or yarn dev; open the generated site; verify the ready text and a secure WebSocket in browser developer tools. Creating channels and the full chat UI comes after that first connected-client signal.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at the tutorial's first observable Stream connection before later channel-list, message, styling, and attachment work.

branches.path: Choose UI components or client SDK for React, iOS/Swift, Android, React Native, Flutter, Angular, Unity, Unreal, or JavaScript, or choose NodeJS, Python, Ruby, PHP, Java, .NET, Go, or REST for the backend. Follow that route's install, app-key, user-token, client initialization, connection, and route-specific rendered or API verification. Stream also offers complete iOS, Android, React Native, and Flutter tutorials, a sample-app install, and an agent-skill route.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes every implementation action and observable signal while retaining a chat connection as the product outcome.

branches.condition: Video & Audio with React custom integration is selected.
branches.path: Choose npm or yarn; install @stream-io/video-react-sdk; obtain an API key, user identity, and token or tokenProvider; create StreamVideoClient once at the app root; create call type default with ID my-first-call; join it with create:true and handle errors; pass it through StreamVideo and StreamCall; render participants with useParticipants and ParticipantView; add a user action before join for autoplay policy; grant camera and microphone permission when required; verify the call reaches JOINED state and participant media renders; leave and disconnect during cleanup.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at a joined and rendered call, with device-permission, autoplay, and connection-error branches absent from non-media routes.

branches.condition: Video & Audio prebuilt, another SDK, Platform API, example app, or use-case tutorial is selected.
branches.path: Choose prebuilt components for a drop-in call lifecycle or custom integration for full control, then select React, iOS/Swift, Android, React Native, Flutter, JavaScript, Unity, Platform API, Vision Agents, or an example app. Within the product choose audio room, live shopping, livestreaming, video calling, voice AI, or voice calling. Complete that route's credential, install, client, call creation or lookup, join, media-permission, render, and route-specific verification steps.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Can reduce UI setup through prebuilt components or replace the client journey entirely with a server/API or sample-app path.

branches.path: Choose client-side or server-side responsibility and an official v3 SDK. For a client example, install the matching package, initialize FeedsClient with the API key, connect user john with a user token, reference the user/john feed, call getOrCreate with watch enabled where supported, add an activity with type post and text Hello, Stream Feeds!, then verify the returned activity or state update. Server-side code is additionally used for feed-group or view configuration, user sync, token return, and writing to another user's feed. If v2 is selected, follow its separate legacy SDK path instead.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at a created feed with its first activity; client/server and v2/v3 choices materially change credentials and package setup.

branches.condition: AI Moderation custom-content checking is selected.
branches.path: Create a Stream app; choose Node, Python, Go, Java, PHP, Ruby, or .NET server SDK; create a moderation policy with rules and actions; initialize the server moderation client with Stream app credentials; submit entity type, entity ID, creator ID, text and/or image payload, policy config key, and optional synchronous-check setting to the moderation Check API; verify the response exposes a recommended keep, flag, remove, or shadow-block action and process that action. Dashboard monitoring, review queue work, and action notifications follow the first recommendation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Ends at an immediate policy-based recommendation rather than a user-facing communication or feed UI.

branches.path: Install uv with Python 3.10 through 3.13; run uvx vision-agents init my-agent and enter the directory; let the scaffold create agent.py, pyproject.toml, .env.example, tests, Dockerfile, and dependencies; create a free Stream account for STREAM_API_KEY and STREAM_API_SECRET; create a Google AI Studio GOOGLE_API_KEY; copy .env.example to .env and fill all three secrets; inspect or customize the generated agent.py; run uv run agent.py run; open the printed browser join link; verify the agent greets the developer after joining the call.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an external Google account and key, Python and uv tooling, three secrets, and a live browser audio interaction. This route alone claims completion in under five minutes.

branches.condition: Stream Agent Skills is selected instead of direct documentation.
branches.path: Install the Stream skill with npx skills add GetStream/agent-skills --skill stream, then invoke the product-specific command such as /stream-react. The router installs a specialist pack when first needed. The generated work must still satisfy the chosen product's credentials, runtime, execution, and observable verification boundary.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Delegates scaffolding to a coding agent but does not remove the product-choice, credential, or runtime gates.

branches.condition: Dashboard onboarding is selected for an own Stream app.
branches.path: Create the account with email and password, Google, or GitHub; select the main product during signup; in the Dashboard select Create application; enter the app name; for Chat or Feeds select the server region and treat it as locked after creation; select development or production mode; submit creation; wait for navigation to the app overview; verify the API key, secret, and product settings are visible.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Exposes every common UI action and makes the irreversible region selection visible before product-specific code.

branches.condition: Stream CLI onboarding is selected on macOS or Linux.
branches.path: Run curl -fsSL https://getstream.io/cli.sh | bash; verify installation with getstream --version; from the project directory run getstream init; complete browser authentication; select the organization; pick an existing app or create one; allow the CLI to write .stream/creds.yaml with app key and secret and add it to .gitignore; choose whether to install Stream skills or select None; verify the local project is linked.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Moves account authentication and app selection or creation into the terminal while still using a browser login and producing local secret material.

branches.condition: The CLI user does not yet want a durable Stream account.
branches.path: Run getstream login --guest before getstream init to create a temporary guest account backed by a throwaway app, then initialize and link the project. Replace the guest state before relying on the app durably.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes immediate durable-account creation but makes the first app intentionally temporary.

branches.condition: An existing Stream application should be reused as the starting point.
branches.path: Choose Clone application in the Dashboard rather than creating an empty app, select the source application, supply the new app's required name and region or mode choices, complete cloning, and open the cloned app overview.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Carries configuration from an existing app into the first-mile state and changes what counts as newly configured work.

branches.condition: Chat Messaging is the selected product and the Build free entitlement applies.
branches.path: Start without a credit card under the Chat Build tier. The current product pricing publishes a 1,000 monthly-active-user allowance, 100 concurrent connections, up to 1 million API calls, up to 100,000 Query Channel calls, and up to 10 GB API bandwidth, with additional Build limits applying to storage and channels.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes a card gate for the Chat first connection while retaining Chat-specific usage ceilings that do not apply to Video or Moderation.

branches.condition: Video & Audio is the selected product and the Build free entitlement applies.
branches.path: Start without a credit card and use the account's $100 per month in free Audio/Video API usage. After those credits are consumed, Stream calculates Video usage monthly according to the product's participant-minute, quality, and optional-feature pricing.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provides a Video-specific monthly credit rather than Chat MAU limits or Moderation content credits.

branches.condition: AI Moderation is the selected product and the Start for Free entitlement applies.
branches.path: Start without a credit card and use $100 in free credits each month across moderation of messages, images, and video, with a Dashboard allowance for three moderators. Beyond the free credit, Pay-As-You-Go rates differ by content type.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provides a Moderation-specific content credit and moderator allowance, separate from Chat and Video entitlements.

friction_gates.description: Five top-level products lead to incompatible outcomes, with no platform-wide default.
Each product adds SDK, UI-versus-client, client-versus-server, version, use-case, sample-app, REST, or agent-route choices.
Credential shape changes by route and can include API key, API secret, user JWT, token provider, and external Google AI key.
Every code route requires language and package tooling appropriate to the selected SDK.
Video and voice routes can require camera and microphone permission plus a user gesture for browser autoplay policy.
Own-app routes require a Stream account and app, while Vision Agents also requires Google AI Studio; the React Chat tutorial can bypass Stream signup using generated credentials.
Common own-app onboarding branches between Dashboard and Stream CLI, with an additional temporary guest-account path in the CLI.

Dashboard app creation requires a name and, for Chat and Feeds, a server region that is locked after creation; the developer also chooses development or production mode and can optionally clone an existing app.

Chat, Video, and Moderation each offer no-card first use, but their entitlements differ and are captured separately: Chat usage limits, Video monthly API credit, and Moderation monthly content credit.

sources.title: Install Stream Agent Skills and CLI

uncertainties.question: Which Stream product defines the workshop's canonical first mile?
uncertainties.impact: The answer changes the success outcome itself, not merely the implementation language.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: Official navigation treats Chat, Video & Audio, Activity Feeds, AI Moderation, and Vision Agents as separate peer products.

uncertainties.question: Within the chosen product, which SDK, UI, server, version, use-case, sample-app, REST, or agent route should be canonical?
uncertainties.impact: The choice changes prerequisites, signup bypass possibilities, credentials, install commands, code, failures, and completion signal.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The product landing pages list multiple routes without one universal default.

uncertainties.question: What exact dynamic signup fields, email-verification screens, organization controls, and anti-abuse checks appear in the current Stream Dashboard?
uncertainties.impact: They may add account or verification actions before the now-documented Dashboard or CLI app-creation sequence.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official getting-started article establishes email/password or social signup, main-product selection, Dashboard and CLI choices, app creation or cloning, immutable region, mode, and overview, but does not enumerate every live account screen.

excluded_after_success: Selecting a canonical Stream product or SDK without workshop-owner direction
Chat channel lists, messages, reactions, threads, uploads, theming, and custom components after the first connection
Video production token service, custom call types, recording, transcription, broadcasting, moderation, and deployment after the first joined call
Feeds follows, ranking, personalization, comments, reactions, push, and migration after the first activity
Moderation review queues, dashboard operations, notifications, integrations, and production enforcement after the first recommendation
Vision Agent voice, video, provider, RAG, telephony, and deployment extensions after the first greeting
Plan limits, production capacity, enterprise contracting, and pricing optimization
